evidence: The problem asks whether A+B 'has positive upper and lower density that are equal', i.e. its natural density; the statement is (A + B).HasPosDensity.
verify_note: decl erdos_125 exists (research solved, AMS 11); QID live, label exact; upper/lower/HasPosDensity is the entire question — load-bearing

evidence: The problem concerns the density of the sumset A+B of the base-3 and base-4 digit-restricted sets.
verify_note: decl exists; QID live, label exact; the problem is precisely about the density of the sumset A+B

evidence: f(n) counts the distinct prime factors of the product of pairwise sums over an n-element set (docstring: 'at least f(n) distinct prime factors').
verify_note: decl erdos_126 exists (research open); QID live, label exact; f(n) counts distinct prime factors — load-bearing

evidence: The problem asks whether every subgraph density condition forces a triangle, i.e. whether G can be triangle-free (formalized as not G.CliqueFree 3).
verify_note: decl erdos_128 exists (research open); QID live, label exact; conclusion is ¬CliqueFree 3, i.e. whether G can be triangle-free

evidence: Docstring: 'Let G be a triangle-free graph on n vertices ... made into a triangle-free graph with diameter 2 by adding edges'.
verify_note: decl erdos_134 exists (research solved); QID live, label exact; hypothesis and conclusion both triangle-free — load-bearing

evidence: The problem asks whether G can be made into a triangle-free graph 'with diameter 2', formalized as every pair of vertices adjacent or joined through a common neighbor.
verify_note: decl exists; QID live, label exact; diameter-2 condition is formalized explicitly (adjacent or common neighbor)

evidence: Docstring: 'Can the product of any k consecutive integers N ever be powerful?', formalized via Nat.Powerful.
verify_note: decl erdos_137 exists (research open); QID live, label exact; statement is ¬N.Powerful — load-bearing

evidence: The decl states the Erdős–Selfridge theorem [ES75] 'The product of consecutive integers is never a power'; Q18027666 is that number-theory theorem (dewiki 'Satz von Erdős-Selfridge' on this diophantine result).
verify_note: decl exists (research solved); QID live, label exact; dewiki 'Satz von Erdős-Selfridge' confirmed a number-theory/diophantine theorem (not the game-theory namesake); decl states ES75 product-of-consecutive-integers-never-a-power exactly

evidence: The variant states the product of k consecutive integers cannot be a perfect power (≠ x^l for l ≥ 2).
verify_note: decl exists; QID live, label exact; conclusion is product ≠ x^l for l≥2 — the perfect-power condition

evidence: The decl asserts that for any r colors and length k some N guarantees a monochromatic arithmetic progression — the docstring calls it 'the fundamental existence result that allows for the definition of the van der Waerden numbers', i.e. van der Waerden's theorem.
verify_note: decl monoAP_guarantee_set_nonempty exists (research solved); QID live, label exact; the statement (∀ r k, ∃ N guaranteeing mono AP) IS van der Waerden's theorem

evidence: The problem asks whether W(k)^{1/k} tends to infinity, where W is the van der Waerden number defined in the file.
verify_note: decl erdos_138 exists (research open); QID live, label exact; W(k) is the van der Waerden number, the object of the limit

evidence: W(k) is the threshold for r-colorings of {1,...,N} to contain a monochromatic arithmetic progression of length k.
verify_note: decl exists; QID live, label exact; monochromatic APs define W(k) — load-bearing

evidence: The decl states r_k(N) = o(N) for the largest AP-free subset of {1,...,N}; erdosproblems.com/139 notes this problem is Szemerédi's theorem (status: proved).
verify_note: decl erdos_139 exists (research solved); QID live, label exact; r_k(N)=o(N) is the density form of Szemerédi's theorem; problems.yaml #139 comment says 'Szemerédi's theorem'

evidence: r_k(N) is the largest subset of {1,...,N} with no non-trivial k-term arithmetic progression.
verify_note: decl exists; QID live, label exact; r_k counts largest AP-free subset — load-bearing

evidence: The problem counts integers representable in exactly one way as a sum of two elements of A (the defining property of Sidon/B_2 sets); erdosproblems.com tags 14 with 'sidon sets'.
